# CDC Program Evaluation Framework

## At a glance

CDC's Program Evaluation Framework guides public health professionals in conducting program evaluation. It is a practical, nonprescriptive tool, designed to summarize and organize essential elements of program evaluation.

## About CDC's Program Evaluation Framework

[CDC's Program Evaluation Framework](/mmwr/volumes/73/rr/rr7306a1.htm?s_cid=rr7306a1_w) provides a guide for designing and conducting evaluation across many programs and settings within and outside public health. It can be used to build evidence, understand programs, and improve evidence-based decision making. The framework consists of:

![Circular graphic with center, middle, and outer rings displaying the standards, steps, and cross-cutting actions of the CDC Evaluation Framework.](/evaluation/media/images/2024/09/Evaluation-Framework-Final-9.3-1.png "CDC Program Evaluation Framework")

### Cross-Cutting Actions

The three [cross-cutting actions](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/about-cross-cutting-actions.html) are the foundational principles of the evaluation framework, and should be woven into each step of an evaluation:

### Evaluation Standards

High quality evaluation should consider these five [evaluation standards](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/about-evaluation-standards.html):

### Evaluation Steps

Each evaluation should follow these steps:

# Step 6 â Act on Findings

## Key points

## Overview and Importance

Facilitating the use of evaluation findings requires planning, collaboration, and commitment from the evaluators and interest holders to act on these findings and recommendations. Since evaluation findings do not translate into action automatically, this step is an essential element in the evaluation cycle.

The important components of this step are:

Refer to the full-length [CDC Program Evaluation Action Guide](/evaluation/media/pdfs/2024/12/FINAL-Action-Guide-for-DFE-12182024_1.pdf) for additional information, examples and worksheets to apply the concepts discussed in this step.

## Planning the Findings for Use

Planning for how the evaluation findings and recommendations will be used happens throughout the evaluation process. For example, when evaluators collaborate with interest holders to develop the evaluation design, questions, and indicators ([Step 3](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework-action-guide/step-3-focus-the-evaluation-questions-and-design.html) and [Step 4](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework-action-guide/step-4-gather-credible-evidence.html)), they also consider the use of the evaluation findings. Questions to consider at each step of the evaluation process to prepare for use of the evaluation findings include:

## Preparing the Findings for Use

Planning ways to communicate evaluation findings and updates throughout each step of the evaluation process helps to build trust between the evaluators and interest holders. This could involve planned periodic discussions to share updates, seek feedback, or to review interim findings.

Existing structured approaches that encourage rapid use of findings throughout the evaluation process include the ****Knowledge-to-Action****[1](#cdcreference_1) and ****Data-to-Action Frameworks****[2](#cdcreference_2). The steps and considerations for how to complete a rapid feedback cycle, based on the Data-to-Action Framework are:

### Communicating Evaluation Findings

There are various ways you can communicate evaluation findings to interest holders. Important communication considerations to keep in mind are:

Timing

The timeframe for when the message will be delivered.

Style

The type of communication style that will be
used when delivering the message. These can be mixed and should
be tailored to each audience.

Tone

The tone of voice (pitch, volume, speed, body language) or tone of writing set the
mood for the message and how it will influence the audience.

Source

The individual who sends the message.

Format

The method that will be used to share the
message with the audience.

Channel

The way that the message will travel between the
sender and receiver. This can be either spoken or written, depending
on the message format that was chosen.

Context

The setting where the message will be delivered,
and the expectations of those who will receive the message.

## Facilitating Insights to Action

Evaluators serve as facilitators for interpreting the findings, uncovering and applying insights, and preventing misuse. Evaluators also are responsible for identifying how to encourage interest holders to use the evaluation findings and how to support the users after they receive the evaluation findings. Possible questions that evaluators can consider are:

## Applying the Cross-Cutting Actions and Evaluation Standards to Step 6

As with all the evaluation framework steps, it is important to integrate the [evaluation standards](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/about-evaluation-standards.html) and [cross-cutting actions](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/about-cross-cutting-actions.html) when acting on findings in Step 6. See [Table 10](/mmwr/volumes/73/rr/rr7306a1.htm?s_cid=rr7306a1_w#T10_down) in the [CDC Program Evaluation Framework, 2024](/mmwr/volumes/73/rr/rr7306a1.htm?s_cid=rr7306a1_w) to determine if you have effectively applied the evaluation standards and [cross-cutting actions](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/about-cross-cutting-actions.html).

# CDC Program Evaluation Framework Action Guide

## Purpose

The Program Evaluation Framework Action Guide serves as a comprehensive resource to assist new or early career evaluators with applying the CDC Program Evaluation Framework across a range of programs and settings. This Action Guide serves as a how-to guide for planning, designing, and implementing evaluations in a practical way.

## Topics Covered

The following foundational objectives are covered in this Action Guide:

![Circular graphic with center, middle, and outer rings displaying the standards, steps, and cross-cutting actions of the CDC Evaluation Framework.](/evaluation/media/images/2024/09/Evaluation-Framework-Final-9.3-1.png "CDC Program Evaluation Framework Graphic")

## How to Use the Action Guide

This guide is a starting point for planning, implementing, and conducting program evaluations. It provides practical applications of the **[CDC Program Evaluation Framework](/evaluation/php/evaluation-framework/index.html)** to any program evaluation. Although this framework is often discussed as a linear process, conducting program evaluation is iterative. This Action Guide is intended to support your current evaluation needs and help you develop robust program evaluations. The guide is separated by each step of the CDC Program Evaluation Framework and can be used as a stand-alone resource.

The following features are included within each step to help with navigation:

For detailed case examples and worksheets that you can adapt for your evaluation, refer to the full-length [CDC Program Evaluation Framework Action Guide](/evaluation/media/pdfs/2024/12/FINAL-Action-Guide-for-DFE-12182024_1.pdf).

## On This Page

enriched_points.expanded_content: The CDC framework consists of six critical steps: engage stakeholders, describe the program, focus evaluation questions, gather credible evidence, justify conclusions, and ensure use of findings.
Evaluation is not linear; it involves revisiting previous steps based on findings, enabling continuous improvement and adaptation throughout the evaluation cycle.
Engaging stakeholders ensures their insights and needs are considered, fostering collaboration and enhancing the relevance and applicability of evaluation outcomes.
